summary: I have lived in Jacksonville, Florida since September 1906, where I have taught music and played in concerts both amateur and professional. Jacksonville was also where I became involved with early radio broadcasting, especially with WJAX, the municipal broadcasting station. My husband was related to a well-known Jacksonville politician, and my son still lives here. The city has been central to my musical career and personal life.

Florida has been my home for many years, especially Jacksonville where I've contributed to the musical scene and radio broadcasting. I recall the glamorous days of Florida before the War Between the States, as remembered by an elderly lady whose songs I recorded. My family connections and my own career have been deeply rooted in this state. Florida's cultural and musical life has been enriched by projects like the Federal Music Project, which I have supported.

Athens, Georgia, is where my mother was born and where I attended public schools and a private girls' school, the Faelton School. I also returned there after studying music in Chicago and Boston. Athens was important in my early education and musical development, and it is connected to my family's history as well.

Heinrich von der Lieth was my maternal grandfather, a German who fled compulsory military training under Kaiser Wilhelm I. He was originally from Hanover, Germany, and his father was tailor to the Emperor of Germany, embroidering royal garments. Heinrich settled in Charleston, South Carolina, where he married my grandmother before moving to Athens, Georgia. His story is part of my family's rich heritage.

Kaiser Wilhelm I was the emperor during the time my grandfather Heinrich von der Lieth fled Germany to avoid compulsory military training. Although my great-grandfather worked as a tailor embroidering the emperor's clothing, Heinrich did not approve of the new military policies under Kaiser Wilhelm I. This historical context influenced my family's migration to America.

Hanover, Germany, was the hometown of my grandfather Heinrich von der Lieth and his father, who was tailor to the Emperor. It is where my family's European roots lie before they emigrated to the United States. This place is significant to my heritage and the stories passed down about my ancestors' lives.

Bolivia, Germany, is where my grandmother was born in 1804. She married my grandfather after he fled Germany and settled in Charleston, South Carolina. This place is part of my maternal lineage and family history.

Charleston, South Carolina, is where my grandfather Heinrich von der Lieth landed after fleeing Germany and where he met and married my grandmother. It was their first home in America before moving to Athens, Georgia. Charleston marks the beginning of my family's American chapter.

James Alfred Grant was my father, whose ancestors came to America on the famous Mayflower. He was connected to notable relatives like Josiah Bartlett, a signer of the Declaration of Independence. My father's family history is a proud part of my heritage, and I have an old book showing Josiah Bartlett's name prominently.

The Mayflower is significant in my family history because my father's ancestors came to America on that famous ship. This connection links me to early American settlers and notable historical figures. It is a source of pride and identity for me.

Josiah Bartlett was one of my father's famous relatives, a signer of the Declaration of Independence. I have a very old book with a reproduction of the Declaration where his name is prominently written. This connection to a founding father is an important part of my family's legacy.

Bartlett Park in Atlanta was named after Leonard Grant, my father's paternal uncle. This park is a beautiful landmark in Georgia and part of my extended family's history. It connects me to the city of Atlanta and my family's influence there.

Atlanta is connected to my family through Leonard Grant, who gave his name to Bartlett Park, a notable city landmark. My sisters live in Atlanta and near the old home place, so the city remains important to my family. Atlanta represents part of my roots and family connections in Georgia.

Nies Caine Oenowski was the Russian lady who ran the private girls' school I attended in Athens, Georgia, called the Faelton School. She was a woman of education and refinement and played a significant role in my early schooling. Her influence helped shape my academic and musical foundation.

Charles Herty was connected to Tharlee Horty, who married Miss Oenowski's niece. Horty was famous for his experiments in the turpentine industry and discoveries related to paper manufacturing from pine. Though not directly related to me, this connection illustrates the notable people linked to my educational circle.

I was awarded a year's scholarship to the Chicago Musical College, where I received wonderful training as a pianist. My time there exposed me to many famous musicians and theatrical people, enriching my musical education. This experience was a key step in my development before I returned to Athens and later studied in Boston.

After Chicago, I went to Boston to enroll in the Faelton Pianoforte School, from which I also graduated. In Boston, I met many distinguished artists and musicians, including Josef Hoffmann, who influenced me greatly. The city was a cultural advantage and helped polish my musical skills through rigorous recitals and concerts.

The Faelton School in Athens, Georgia, was where I completed my regular grades and studied music. Under the direction of Nies Caine Oenowski, the school had an especially fine music department. I practiced six hours a day there, which prepared me well for my later studies and career.

I knew Josef Hoffmann personally during my studies in Boston, and his kind interest and advice influenced me greatly. Meeting such a famous European musician was an important part of my artistic development. His guidance helped shape my approach to music and performance.

While in Chicago, I met Joseph Jefferson, a famous theatrical interpreter. My exposure to the theatrical element there was strong, and I encountered many notable figures in theater and music. Jefferson's reputation and presence added to the rich cultural environment that influenced me.

Richard Mansfield was renowned for his portrayal of Mephistopheles in Faust, and I became acquainted with him during my time in Chicago. His theatrical work was part of the artistic circle I encountered, which blended music and theater. This exposure enriched my appreciation for performance arts.

Faust is the play in which Richard Mansfield famously portrayed Mephistopheles, a role I admired during my time in Chicago. The theatrical productions I witnessed, including Faust, were like high-class concerts blending music and drama. These experiences influenced my artistic sensibilities.

WJAX was the municipal broadcasting station in Jacksonville where I performed as an accompanist starting in 1926. I played for Henry Cornely and his associates, contributing to early radio music programs. Being part of WJAX allowed me to embrace the new possibilities of radio in distributing music to the public.

The Florida Times-Union building in Jacksonville was near the old shed where the first little radio station was established, which later evolved into WJAX. This proximity connected me to the early days of radio broadcasting in the city. The Times-Union was part of the environment that fostered my involvement in radio music.

Henry Cornely was a bandleader in Jacksonville for whom I played as accompanist on several radio programs at WJAX. I worked with him and his associates, including Professor LePaige, during the early years of radio broadcasting. This collaboration was a significant part of my musical career in Jacksonville.

I greatly admire President Franklin D. Roosevelt and believe his administration will be remembered as the greatest humanitarian effort ever. I support his policies wholeheartedly and hope he will serve a third term. The Federal Music Project, established during his administration, has been a vital support for musicians like myself.
